mnemonics.title: Matrix Size
mnemonics.content: n² = 'Never Two' - always square, n rows and n columns for n vertices

mnemonics.title: Symmetric Property
mnemonics.content: Undirected = 'U' looks like symmetric shape; Matrix mirrors across diagonal

mnemonics.title: Row = Out, Column = In
mnemonics.content: Row 'leaves' the vertex (out), Column 'comes to' the vertex (in)

mnemonics.title: Edge Check O(1)
mnemonics.content: A[i][j] = 1: Instant access like array indexing - 'One-look' operation

mnemonics.title: Dense vs Sparse
mnemonics.content: Dense = 'Dense' = Dense packing in n²; Sparse = 'Spare' = Spare/extra space wasted in matrix

acronyms.expansion: Entry at row i, column j in adjacency matrix A
acronyms.usage: Represents edge between vertex i and j (or from i to j for digraphs)
Space required and time to find all neighbors in adjacency matrix
Adjacency matrix has all 1s except diagonal (for simple graphs)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine folding the matrix along main diagonal - both halves match perfectly
Think of each row as 'rays of light' emanating from a center point (vertex)
Think of each column as 'funnels' collecting incoming edges to a vertex
Dense = most cells filled (dark matrix); Sparse = mostly empty (light matrix with few filled cells)
A² shows connections through one intermediate - like jumping to one stop before destination

analogies.analogy: Like a distance matrix between cities - each intersection has a value showing connection/distance
Like checking all doors from one room to find connected rooms - check each door (row entry)
Two-way street - if street A connects to B, then B connects to A; matrix mirrors this
Like looking up contact in phone book by direct address (page/row) - instant access
Like a full subway map with connections between most stations - matrix mostly filled
